questions.text: I routinely dedicate my best hours to my own creative and professional projects.
I believe that clear, direct speech is a sign of respect.
I keep important documents filed and easy to locate at a moment's notice.
I believe clarity of vision matters more than everyone's agreement.
I am at my best when I am a trusted co-pilot.
I adjust naturally as I go rather than setting aside formal review periods.
I notice when someone is telling a story primarily to position themselves in a certain light.
I hold the view that I should always be looking for ways to lighten someone else's burden.
When I feel gratitude, it swells inside me until I can barely contain it.
I believe that the professionals and leaders who make the greatest positive impact are those who can process suffering without emotional disruption.
I tend to weigh how a decision affects me before considering how it affects others.
I rarely know what I'll be doing tomorrow until tomorrow comes.
I can distinguish between understanding that someone is in pain and actually feeling that pain myself — and I tend toward the former.
I hold the view that the moral significance of suffering should be the primary consideration when I am deciding how to act.
I notice that excitement in me feels like a pleasant warmth rather than a roaring fire.
I feel more comfortable in positions without formal authority.
I usually do not register the social significance of small talk.
I tend to move through emotionally charged moments with a sense of groundedness.
